title: Making a leather key fob
subtitle: Cut, punch, edge, and assemble a solid leather key fob with a split ring fitting
excerpt: How to make a key fob from a piece of vegetable-tanned leather. Covers cutting the blank, punching the slot and holes, bevelling the edges, and burnishing to a finished edge.

glossaryTerms.definition: Leather tanned using plant-based tannins (oak bark, mimosa) rather than chrome salts. Vegetable-tan is stiffer, takes impressions and burnishing well, and develops a patina with use. The standard material for leatherwork projects. Chrome-tanned leather is softer and harder to burnish.

Wetting a cut leather edge with water or gum tragacanth, then rubbing briskly with a burnisher until the fibres compress and the edge polishes to a hard smooth surface. Only works on vegetable-tanned leather.

content.text: A key fob is a good first leatherwork project. The piece is small, the cuts are straight, and the finishing sequence (bevel, wet, burnish) is the same one used on every project that follows. Use
at 2 to 3 mm thick. This is firm enough to hold its shape and thin enough to cut cleanly.
Step 1, Mark and cut the blank

Mark a rectangle 90 × 25 mm on the grain side of the leather with the combination square and a pencil. Cut along the lines with the leatherwork knife against a straight metal rule on a cutting mat. A single firm pull along the rule is cleaner than a sawing motion, hesitation at corners leaves a ragged edge. Check the blank is square: a rhombus-shaped fob is difficult to finish cleanly.

Mark the centre of one short end at 12 mm from the end. Use a strap-end punch or two overlapping chisel punches to create an oval slot 8 × 3 mm. Thread a 25 mm split ring through the slot, the ring must pass through easily without tearing the leather. If the slot is too tight, widen it by a half-millimetre each time until the ring moves freely.

along every cut edge at 45°, holding the tool at a constant angle. A consistent bevel makes the subsequent burnishing faster and the finished edge more even. Run the beveller around the punched slot as well. Remove the fine shaving of leather produced by the bevel before burnishing.
Step 4, Wet and burnish the edges
Dampen the cut edge with a small amount of water applied on the fingertip, the edge should darken but not be soaking. Run the grooved face of the

back and forth along the edge briskly, pressing into the groove. The friction from speed is what polishes the fibres, slow rubbing achieves nothing. The edge will turn from rough to hard and shiny in 30 to 60 seconds. Burnish all four long edges and both short ends. Thread the split ring through the slot to complete.
